name: Budapest: Heights of the Buda Castle District

stops.name: Széchenyi Chain Bridge
stops.shortDescription: Opened in 1849, this was the first permanent bridge across the Danube River connecting Buda and Pest. It is guarded by massive stone lions that mysteriously lack tongues.

stops.name: Budapest Castle Hill Funicular
stops.shortDescription: Operating since 1870, this vintage funicular railway takes passengers up the steep incline from the riverbank to the castle. The wooden, stepped carriages provide excellent views during the short ascent.

stops.name: Buda Castle
stops.shortDescription: The historical castle and palace complex of the Hungarian kings, first completed in 1265. It has been destroyed and rebuilt numerous times, mostly recently after the heavy siege of Budapest in World War II.

stops.name: Sándor Palace
stops.shortDescription: A beautiful Neoclassical building that serves as the official residence and workspace of the President of Hungary. The ceremonial changing of the guards takes place in the square right in front.

stops.name: Matthias Church
stops.shortDescription: A spectacular late-Gothic church topped with diamond-patterned Zsolnay ceramic tiles. It served as a coronation church for Hungarian kings and was briefly converted into a mosque during the Ottoman occupation.

stops.name: Fisherman's Bastion
stops.shortDescription: A fairy-tale viewing terrace constructed in the late 19th century. Its seven distinctive stone towers represent the seven Magyar tribes who founded the Hungarian nation in the 9th century.

stops.name: Holy Trinity Column
stops.shortDescription: A highly ornate Baroque plague column located in the central square of the Castle District. It was erected in the 18th century by grateful survivors of the devastating Black Death.

stops.name: Labyrinth of Buda Castle
stops.shortDescription: A vast underground network of natural caves and cellars deep beneath the castle. Famously, Vlad the Impaler (the historical inspiration for Dracula) was once held prisoner here.

stops.name: Hospital in the Rock
stops.shortDescription: A fascinating underground hospital hidden in the natural cave system. It was used extensively during WWII and the 1956 Revolution, later converted into a classified nuclear bunker.

stops.name: Tóth Árpád Promenade
stops.shortDescription: A picturesque, tree-lined promenade running along the western ramparts of the Castle District. It offers beautiful views over the Buda hills, away from the heavy tourist crowds.

stops.name: Mary Magdalene Tower
stops.shortDescription: The solitary surviving bell tower of a 13th-century Gothic church that was destroyed in WWII. Visitors can climb to the top for a spectacular panoramic view of Budapest.

stops.name: Museum of Military History
stops.shortDescription: Housed in a former army barracks, this museum chronicles Hungary's tumultuous military history. Its courtyard features an impressive collection of historic cannons and artillery.

stops.name: National Archives of Hungary
stops.shortDescription: An imposing Neo-Romanesque building near the northern gate, easily recognizable by its spectacular, brightly colored Zsolnay ceramic tile roof that rivals Matthias Church.

stops.name: Vienna Gate (Bécsi Kapu)
stops.shortDescription: The historic northern entrance to the heavily fortified Castle District. Historically, it connected the city of Buda to the vital trade and travel routes leading towards Vienna.
